title: What Does Being a Person Who Sleeps Well Feel Like
subtitle: A journal practice for stepping into a new sleep identity
excerpt: A set of journal prompts for Day 10 of the sleep arc. The writing works with the identity question, not 'how do I sleep better' but 'who is the version of me who already does'. The prompts move from the old story to the new one, and end with a sentence written from that version.
sourceNotes: Theme from Day 10 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive to Fall Asleep Faster and Wake Energised (Rebecca J Page, 2025, ISBN 9798269649795). Prompt structure original to homemade.education.

content.text: Journal prompts for Day 10 of the sleep arc. Use them in the evening, sitting up. The prompts work with a specific question: not how to sleep better, but who the version of you is who already does.
New to journal prompts as a practice? Read
journal prompts as practice
first.
The practice
Warm-up
What has your story about sleep said about you?
Write the story freely, 'I'm a bad sleeper', 'I've always struggled', whatever words come. Get them on the page without editing.
What is different now compared to when you started this work?
Write what has shifted, even small things. What does your body do differently? What thoughts have changed?
Closer in
What would it feel like to be a person who sleeps well without effort?
Not what it would look like, what it would feel like. Write from the body. Morning energy, evening ease, the absence of dread.
What would change if sleep was no longer a problem for you?
Write what opens up, work, relationships, energy, mornings. Concrete changes, not general ones.
Write one sentence as the version of you who already sleeps well.
Start with 'I am..' and write it as present fact. Not a wish, a statement.
Where this practice comes from
Built from the Day 10 theme of
SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive
(Rebecca J Page, 2025): anchoring calm as identity rather than achievement. Prompt structure original to homemade.education.
